title: Vodka Infusion Time Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How long to infuse vodka with fruits & spices
description: Calculate optimal vodka infusion time for fruits, herbs & spices. DIY limoncello: citrus 3–5 days, berries 2–4 days, spices 2–4 weeks.
intro: **Infusing vodka** with fruit, herbs, or spices creates delicious homemade liqueurs: limoncello (citrus), vanilla vodka, jalapeño vodka, coffee vodka. **Timing is key**: citrus 3–7 days, soft fruits 2–4 days, spices 1–4 weeks. This calculator gives you the optimal infusion time by ingredient and recommended fruit-to-vodka ratio.
keyTakeaway: **Citrus (lemon, orange)**: 3–5 days. **Soft fruits (strawberry, blueberry)**: 2–4 days. **Spices (vanilla, cinnamon)**: 2–4 weeks. Typical fruit-to-vodka ratio: 1:3.
useCases: You want to make homemade limoncello but aren't sure how long to wait.
You have leftover fruit and want to infuse it into vodka.
You're planning to gift infused spirits and need a production timeline.
You're making vanilla vodka for cocktails.
You're experimenting with exotic infusions (jalapeño, tea, lavender).

example.title: Example: Homemade limoncello with 750ml vodka
steps: **Time**: 4–5 days in a dark glass jar.
**Result**: 1.5L of limoncello ~25% ABV ready for freezer.
example.result: 4–5 days infusion + straining + syrup = 1.5L homemade limoncello ready in 1 week total.

explanation:
## Infusion principles

Alcohol **extracts compounds** both fat-soluble (essential oils, aromatics) and water-soluble (sugars, acids). More time = more extraction, but overdoing it turns bitter or introduces unwanted tannins.

## Optimal infusion times

| Ingredient | Time | Ratio per 750ml |
|---|---|---|
| **Limoncello (citrus)** | **3–5 days** | **Zest of 6–8 lemons** |
| Orange | 4–6 days | Zest of 4–5 oranges |
| Strawberry | 3–4 days | 300–400g |
| Blueberry, raspberry | 3–5 days | 300g |
| Peach | 5–7 days | 4–5 peaches |
| Apple | 7–10 days | 3–4 apples |
| **Vanilla** | **2–4 weeks** | **3–5 pods** |
| Coffee beans | 5–7 days | 100g roasted |
| Cinnamon | 1–2 weeks | 3–4 sticks |
| Clove | 5–7 days | 10–15 pieces |
| Black peppercorn | 1 week | 20–30 grains |
| Fresh mint | 3–4 days | 30–40 leaves |
| Rosemary | 1–2 weeks | 3 sprigs |
| Jalapeño | 2–4 days | 2–3 whole |
| Ginger | 1–2 weeks | 50g peeled & grated |
| Black tea | 1–3 days | 4–5 tea bags |


See also [whisky dilution for target ABV](/whisky-dilution-target-abv) and [kombucha fermentation time](/kombucha-fermentation-time).

faq.q: What happens if I over-infuse vodka?
faq.a: With citrus and soft fruits, don't—they turn bitter and oxidize. Hard spices can infuse for months without issue.

faq.q: Should I use premium vodka or budget vodka?
faq.a: Mid-quality neutral vodka works perfectly. The infused ingredient masks the spirit's character, so premium isn't necessary.

faq.q: Can I use frozen fruit for infusions?
faq.a: Yes, frozen fruit actually works better. Freezing bursts cell walls, extracting faster. Use the same ratio as fresh fruit.

faq.q: How long does finished infusion keep?
faq.a: Strained and sweetened: 1–2 years refrigerated. Unsweetened: indefinitely in a dark place at room temperature.

faq.q: Can I infuse multiple ingredients at once?
faq.a: Yes, but use the shortest infusion time (the most delicate ingredient). Example: cinnamon + strawberry → 3 days (berries oxidize after).
Vinegar preserves well and extracts flavor. Vegetable glycerin extracts differently (sweeter result). Both are alcohol-free options.

faq.q: What's the best container for infusions?
faq.a: Use dark glass jars kept away from direct sunlight. UV light degrades flavor and color over time.

faq.q: Do I need to shake the jar during infusion?
faq.a: Light daily shaking helps extraction, but it's not required. Just store at room temperature away from sunlight.
